author: @wimwiddershins
text:
In my own designs, I try to get multiple functions from each part = Part reduction & increased function.
For example the tyres of a car perform several functions; Road gripping, shock absorbing, allow for limitations of steering/drive geometry, hard wearing replaceable interface between car & environment...
Probably others?

author: @Dave_D.
text: The twisted screw could be made to run any size coil as well....just make interchangeable twisted screws, then simply swap it out for the one that matches your coil.  As long as you could make a design where it is easy to change the length of the holder and install the different length screws.

author: @DavidD6969

author: @jennywoodfield9951
text: My Nan had a Singer sewing machine operated by a foot treadle. The bobbin winding mechanism always fascinated me with it’s beautiful simplicity. It was a rod attached to a squashed heart-shaped cam. It wound the thread onto the bobbin with such precision and elegance.

author: @eugenesands5665
text: Hi Robert. Love your videos. If you change the angle of the reversible screw compared to the spindle then the length of travel can change. One size can fit all spindles within reason. Eugene.

author: @deslomeslager
text:
I would go for reversing the polarity to the motor.
Do note: I find this device you have there a little bit like what I found as a child in an electric car. I opened it up to see why going backwards rotates the front wheels, but not when you go forwards. I found a rotating part, like the one you have, only this one was 'switching' using friction. Thus when you reverse polarity the motor spins the other way, and by friction it pushes that middle gear towards the other part of the mechanism. That same friction also 'kept' pushing the gears together. It was a nifty way to add 'steering' to a car with only 2 wires from the 'wired remote control'.

author: @McRootbeer
text: It's great to hear the explanation of why you make the design choices you do. And if you could roll in some close up footage of the mechanism that would be great too 😅.

author: @andrewsmithmilan1780

author: @ThatTweedle
text:
OH THIS LOOKS TO BE A REALLY FUN PROJECT TO WATCH BEING DESIGNED AND BUILT (oops, sorry about the caps_) 
I just love seeing machines being built... well.. with someone explaining it to me lol..
I just found a guy's channel ( called 'Joshua De Lisle' ) where he does kinda same thing, but he uses a lot of welding/metal work PLUS 3d-printing too... He makes his own tools too LOL Like he made his own CNC plasma cutter out of a $150 cheapo laser engraver/cutter!!! And he's built all these other typically expensive machines and is using them to put together a steam powered generator to power his workshop using WOOD as the fuel LOL - I just love out-of-the-box ideas like that and THAT is EXACTLY what drew me to your channel Robert!
So I can't wait to see how this is all put together, it sounds BRILLIANT so far! Maybe you'll come up with a machine that could wind those awesome serpentine coils for pancake motors... :)

author: @rfiskillingussoftly6568
text: Brings to mind a cart with sliding bumper...the cart moves till it hits the bumper, the bumper slides in...the cart changes direction, the bumper runs through the entire cart...so as the front bumper slides in,the back bumper slides out...the cart runs till the back bumper hits and slides in..pushing the front bumper out ready for the next hit ...making the cart go back and forth...get what Im saying lol! Simple concept, challenge to describe!  Great video!

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
You can build a reverser like they use on some machine tools where there is a trip on each end, on a bar which allows one to space the trip along the bar on each end [basically a pair of opposed trips, each for one end] and these trips only exist for the purpose of precisely engaging the reversing of the shuttle.

The shuttle has a rocker on it that, when tripped, instantly reverses the direction of travel.

author: @koaasst
text: like an open face fishing reel, cool to see a big representation of the level wind

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
I have a coil winder that has a counter, a speed controller and is reversible with a mounting point where I can mount various mandrels and holder shapes, with a feed reel with controlled resistance.

The only part I lack at this point, is the wire feed needle on a synchronized shuttle.

But I have the parts to build one, it just has not yet become a priority for any of the coils I have needed to wind.

I do plan some day to build and mount it eventually.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
What if you just had three gears, two side by side meshing the 3rd sliding between them as they spread apart just enough to no longer mesh, or* 
Have two sets of gears, one with with 2meshing gears, one with (3)three, fir reverse, have the drive  
Driving both, the output side on bearings with a sliding dog between keyed to the shaft,  a simple slide could be a fraction of mm and it reversed! 👍
dogs rive

author: @grendel1960a
text: I can think of several even simpler mechanisms to reverse the threading on a coil winder, the simplest is a guide that is positioned on a horizontal circular turntable place it near the edge and the turning of the turntable will space a wider coil, nearer the centre for a shorter coil, drive this using a worm from the main winding motor and it will traverse the coil say every hundred turns of the coil (dependent upon gearing). if you want a linear motion of the guide use a scotch yoke to transfer to a wire guide on the end of the yoke.

author: @NiteLynr
text: I'm hoping that this component is going to be leading to a series on building a full variable coil-winder. As always, loving your approach to development and iterative design. Also, as an added bonus, I get to see gzumwalt being referenced, another of my favourite designers, if mostly for knick knacks and novelties.

author: @johnbarry8185
text:
Rob, I think his second version is decidedly better.  It is smaller, uses less parts, and your wire rolling rod can be easily attached to the gear with the indicator.  That being said, wouldn't your magnet transmission work better?  That would be neat to see in a machine design like this.
Keep up the good work.
Godspeed.

text: Hi I have & use a treadle sewing machine that uses a shuttle & that uses a gear & cam mechanism to move the cotton along the length of the shuttle bobbin & is quite fascinating to watch it filling the bobbin & the rest of the machine is fascinating as well.
